is this fact correct Tumor antigenic peptides bind to Hsp70 and then internalized by APCs to trigger their cross-presentation through immune effector cells

Yes, this statement is generally correct. Tumor antigenic peptides can bind to Hsp70 (Heat shock protein 70) and are then internalized by Antigen Presenting Cells (APCs) to trigger a process called cross-presentation. This process allows the APCs to present the tumor antigens to immune effector cells, such as T cells, leading to an immune response against the tumor.

Paraphrased version: Hsp70 can function as both a source and a carrier of tumor antigenic peptides, facilitating their internalization by APCs for cross-presentation and subsequent activation of immune effector cells.
diverse immunogenic functions
Wide-ranging immunogenic functions

write me an article on immunogenic function of extracellular hsp70 based on its functions as danger signal to innate immunity, chaperone function, cross-presentation, T-cell stimulation and immunosuppressive agent.

Title: The Diverse Immunogenic Functions of Extracellular Hsp70 in Innate Immunity

Introduction:
Extracellular Hsp70 (Heat shock protein 70) is known for its diverse immunogenic functions that play critical roles in immune responses. This article explores the various ways by which extracellular Hsp70 acts as a danger signal to innate immunity, a chaperone, a facilitator of cross-presentation, a stimulator of T-cells, and even as an immunosuppressive agent.

The Danger Signal:
Extracellular Hsp70 acts as a danger signal, alerting the immune system to cellular stress and damage. Under normal conditions, Hsp70 is predominantly localized within the cell. However, during cellular stress, such as exposure to heat, toxins, or infection, Hsp70 is released into the extracellular space. This extracellular Hsp70 acts as a potent danger signal, recognized by pattern recognition receptors (PRRs) such as toll-like receptors (TLRs) on innate immune cells. This recognition triggers a cascade of immune responses, including the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines, recruitment of immune cells, and activation of antigen-presenting cells (APCs).

Chaperone Function:
Apart from its role as a danger signal, extracellular Hsp70 also functions as a chaperone for extracellular proteins involved in immune responses. It assists in the proper folding and stabilization of proteins, ensuring their functional integrity. By acting as a chaperone, Hsp70 promotes antigen processing and presentation by APCs. This helps in the recognition and activation of immune effector cells such as T-cells, leading to an amplified immune response against pathogens or tumor cells.

Cross-Presentation:
Extracellular Hsp70 plays a crucial role in cross-presentation, a process by which APCs present antigens from extracellular sources to stimulate specific immune responses. Antigenic peptides from tumors or pathogens can bind to Hsp70, forming stable complexes that are internalized by APCs. This facilitates the cross-presentation of antigens to CD8+ T-cells, leading to the activation of cytotoxic T-lymphocytes (CTLs) and ultimately, the destruction of tumor cells or infected cells.

T-Cell Stimulation:
In addition to cross-presentation, extracellular Hsp70 also directly stimulates T-cell responses. Hsp70 can interact with cell surface receptors on T-cells, triggering their activation, proliferation, and production of immune mediators. This direct interaction with T-cells is crucial for generating adaptive immunity against pathogens and tumors.

Immunosuppressive Agent:
Interestingly, extracellular Hsp70 can also exhibit immunosuppressive properties under certain conditions. In certain contexts, Hsp70 has been shown to inhibit immune cell activation and cytokine production, dampening the immune response. This immunosuppressive function of Hsp70 suggests a potential role in regulating immune homeostasis and preventing excessive inflammatory responses.

Conclusion:
Extracellular Hsp70 exhibits a range of immunogenic functions, acting as a danger signal to innate immunity, a chaperone, facilitating cross-presentation, stimulating T-cell responses, and even exerting immunosuppressive effects. The versatile nature of extracellular Hsp70 highlights its significance in modulating immune responses and underscores its potential as a therapeutic target in various diseases, including infections and cancer. Further research and understanding of the immunogenic functions of extracellular Hsp70 will shed light on its precise mechanisms and enable the development of novel strategies to harness its immunomodulatory potential.

danger signal to which immune cells

innate system involved which immune cells

The innate immune system involves various immune cells, including macrophages, dendritic cells, natural killer cells, neutrophils, and innate lymphoid cells. These cells play essential roles in detecting and responding to danger signals, such as Hsp60, in order to initiate the innate immune response.
maturation of bone marrow-derived dendritic cells (BMDCs) via TLR4 and activation of allogeneic T cells.is categorised under which immune response

The maturation of bone marrow-derived dendritic cells (BMDCs) via TLR4 and subsequent activation of allogeneic T cells falls under the category of adaptive immune response. Dendritic cells, as antigen-presenting cells, play a crucial role in presenting antigens to T cells and initiating adaptive immune responses. The activation of T cells by matured BMDCs is a central event in adaptive immunity, leading to the generation of specific immune responses against foreign antigens.

Interestingly, Feng et al. also demonstrated that the increased surface expressions of Hsp60 and Hsp72 were more effective in stimulating the activation/maturation of DC and ultimately, the generation of potent antitumor T-cell responses.

Interestingly, Feng et al. also found that increased surface expressions of Hsp60 and Hsp72 were particularly effective in stimulating the activation and maturation of dendritic cells (DCs). This, in turn, led to the generation of potent antitumor T-cell responses.
